name: Bilderberg Members
description: The Bilderberg Group is an annual, invitation-only conference of approximately 120-150 political leaders, academics, business figures, and journalists, primarily from Europe and North America. It was established in 1954 to foster dialogue between these regions, particularly regarding transatlantic relations. The group meets in secret, under the Chatham House Rule, meaning participants are free to use the information received, but neither the identity nor the affiliation of the speaker(s) nor of any other participant may be revealed. Conspiracy theories surrounding the Bilderberg Group claim it is a shadow government aiming to establish a New World Order, manipulating global events to consolidate power and wealth in the hands of a select elite. Proponents point to the secrecy of the meetings and the high-profile attendees as evidence of nefarious intent. They often cite perceived policy changes following Bilderberg meetings as proof of the group's influence. Mainstream perspective views the Bilderberg Group as a forum for informal discussion and networking among influential individuals. Critics argue that the secrecy surrounding the meetings is undemocratic and allows for undue influence on policy decisions, regardless of whether it's intentional conspiracy or simply the result of shared interests among powerful people. Scholarly analysis often focuses on the sociological aspects of elite gatherings and the potential for agenda-setting, without necessarily endorsing conspiratorial interpretations. The lack of transparency remains a central point of contention.
subtitle: Elite networking or shadow government?

claims.evidence: Secrecy of meetings, powerful attendees, policy changes after meetings.
claims.criticism: Correlation does not equal causation; policy changes are complex and influenced by many factors.

claims.claim: Bilderberg Group aims to establish a New World Order.
claims.evidence: Statements from attendees interpreted as supportive of global governance, promotion of specific policies.
claims.criticism: Interpretation of statements is subjective; policies promoted are often mainstream economic or political ideas.

claims.claim: The Group operates undemocratically due to its secrecy.
claims.evidence: Meetings are closed to the public and press; discussions are not recorded or made public.
claims.criticism: Private discussions allow for more open and honest dialogue; Chatham House Rule is common in high-level discussions.

keyFigures.name: Daniel Estulin
keyFigures.role: Prominent conspiracy theorist and author on the Bilderberg Group.

keyFigures.name: David Rothkopf
keyFigures.role: Author who has written about the global power elite, offering a non-conspiratorial perspective.

mythologyConnections: Themes of secret societies influencing world events, similar to ancient mystery cults.
Allusions to powerful cabals in folklore.

timeline.event: First Bilderberg meeting held in Oosterbeek, Netherlands.
Annual Bilderberg meetings continue, generating speculation and conspiracy theories.
